description_long:
Rowfern applies the pull-request discipline of software engineering to data pipelines. The failure modes it targets are the quiet ones — broken schemas, silent nulls, volume anomalies — that traditionally surface downstream in a dashboard rather than upstream in review. The product's stated ambition is to move the catch from the postmortem to the pull request.

Mechanically, Rowfern reads a dbt project or SQL pipelines and generates checks: schema contracts between producing and consuming models, freshness and volume expectations learned from history, and hand-written assertions in YAML. The checks run in CI on every pull request against a sampled snapshot of production data, so a column rename or a join that fans out is caught before merge. The same checks then run on schedule in production and page the owning team through PagerDuty or Slack when a contract breaks — one definition of correctness enforced in both places.

The business is open core. The check engine is open source under an Apache 2.0 license, while the commercial platform layers on the contract catalog, ownership routing, historical baselines, and SSO. Cloud plans start at $500 per month per workspace and scale with models under contract, with the free engine as the adoption funnel.

Traction is unusually specific for a company this size: data platform teams at Chartbeam, Densify Analytics, and Copperline Health run Rowfern on more than 4,000 models. The site is also candid about the sales motion — teams adopt it after a visible data outage, with a broken dashboard in a board meeting cited as a common origin story.

Founded in Denver in 2022 by two former data engineers, Rowfern is 19 people distributed across US time zones. The wedge is the contract, not the check: plenty of tools can assert that a column is non-null, but binding producers and consumers to an enforced schema in CI is a workflow claim — that data teams will review pipeline changes the way software teams review code.
